costs: Substantial and sustained public investment in teacher incentives and facilities, competing with other fiscal priorities
Long time horizon before demand-side effects on selective-track enrollment materialize, if at all
Does not directly address existing segregation among already-enrolled selective-track cohorts
content.equity_impact: Positive if effective: targets the schools serving the most disadvantaged students directly, potentially narrowing the 71%-of-variance between-school gap over time [F-live-hungarian_education_system-03]. However, effect on the selective tracks' segregation dynamics is indirect and unproven, and demographic headwinds may offset gains [F-live-demography-02, F-live-demography-07].
expected_benefits: Addresses a root-cause driver of selective-track demand rather than only its symptoms, per identified sequencing/precondition problem [F-live-implementation_planning-07]
Directly targets teacher shortage and quality gaps concentrated in rural/disadvantaged schools [F-live-school_network_planning-07]
Avoids immediate political conflict over track structure, sidestepping veto constituencies [F-live-political_feasibility-05]
content.goal: Address the root driver of demand for selective tracks—distrust in local comprehensive school quality—by investing directly in comprehensive schools, as a precondition for any future structural change.

implementation_steps.action: Design targeted teacher-recruitment and retention incentive package for rural and disadvantaged comprehensive schools
Allocate capital investment for facility upgrades in comprehensive schools with under-80 pupils facing closure risk, where mergers are not the chosen path
Track parental school-choice patterns and satisfaction longitudinally to assess whether quality investment reduces demand for early selective-track exit

mechanisms: Teacher shortages and unqualified-teacher concentration in disadvantaged/rural schools are 2-3x higher than in non-disadvantaged schools, directly constraining comprehensive-school quality [F-live-school_network_planning-07]

Sequencing logic: selective-track demand stems from distrust in local school quality, implying quality investment could reduce demand-side pressure for early selection [F-live-implementation_planning-07]

Attendance at high-poverty schools is associated with lower reading scores and secondary completion, indicating direct returns to quality investment in these schools [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-04]
risks: 270 schools already at risk of closure due to demographic decline; investment may be undermined by structural depopulation trends outpacing quality improvements [F-live-demography-07, F-live-demography-02]

No guarantee that improved comprehensive-school quality reduces selective-track demand, since track access also functions as status-signaling and family strategy, not solely a quality-driven choice [F-live-demography-05, F-live-legal_and_governance-05]
Central government's KLIK-driven capacity-planning constraints may limit effective targeted resource allocation [F-live-school_network_planning-06]
content.title: Preserve tracks, expand comprehensive-school quality investment (compensatory strategy)
